title: Box Office: ‘The Odyssey’ Stuns With $124 Million Opening Weekend, Third-Biggest Debut of the Year
description: It’s a box office debut fit for a king. Christopher Nolan‘s action epic “The Odyssey” ignited to $124.5 million from 3,919 domestic theaters over th
body:
It’s a box office debut fit for a king. Christopher Nolan‘s action epic “The Odyssey” ignited to $124.5 million from 3,919 domestic theaters over the weekend, defying expectations and setting up the R-rated spectacle for a long, long journey in theaters. 

	These ticket sales are notable as Nolan’s biggest debut since 2012’s “The Dark Knight Rises” ($160 million) as well as the third-largest opening of the year following “Toy Story 5” ($159 million) and “The Super Mario Galaxy Movie” ($131 million). They’re also impressive not just because “The Odyssey” is a nearly three-hour sword-and-sandal adventure, a genre that’s rarely made these days, but also because it is rated R, which limits who can buy tickets. 
	

	At the international box office, “The Odyssey” collected $139.6 million from 73 markets for a stellar worldwide tally of $264.1 million. This marks Nolan’s biggest global opening ahead of “The Dark Knight Rises” ($249 million, not adjusted for inflation), 2009’s “The Dark Knight” ($198 million, not adjusted for inflation) and “Oppenheimer” ($180 million). 


	Adapted from Homer’s 3,000-year-old epic poem, “The Odyssey” stars Matt Damon as Odysseus, the king of Ithaca, and chronicles his seemingly eternal, perilous voyage home after the Trojan war. Damon leads the all-star ensemble of Tom Holland, Anne Hathaway, Robert Pattinson, Lupita Nyong’o, Zendaya, Charlize Theron, Elliot Page, Jon Bernthal and John Leguizamo. Written and directed by Nolan, “The Odyssey” is one of the best-reviewed films of the year with an “A” grade on CinemaScore exit polls and 95% from critics on Rotten Tomatoes. Males accounted for 56% of ticket buyers while half of audience members were between the ages of 18 and 34, underscoring that Nolan appeals to all generations of moviegoers.
	

	With stellar word-of-mouth, the frenzy around premium formats and an A-list cast that’s been taking the promotional circuit by storm, “The Odyssey” is primed for an epic box office run. Plus, it won’t have any competition until Sony’s “Spider-Man: Brand New Day” opens on July 31. That’s necessary to justify the film’s massive price tag. Universal spent $250 million to produce — a staggering cost for any tentpole these days, especially one that’s rated R — and another $125 million to market to global audiences. 

	“Christopher Nolan is the only filmmaker working today who could take an ancient story like this, attract a cast as sprawling and accomplished as this, and make it a pop culture event,” says David A. Gross, who publishes the box office newsletter FranchiseRe. “Nolan has become a unique and singular talent, with a loyal and enthusiastic following.”

	Nolan has long been a commercial draw, having directed outsized box office triumphs like “The Dark Knight” trilogy, “Inception,” “Dunkirk” and “Interstellar.” But the British filmmaker reached a new level of fame after 2023’s “Oppenheimer,” which became a box office juggernaut before securing the Oscar for best picture. Fueled by the “Barbenheimer” phenomenon, “Oppenheimer” launched above expectations to $82 million and earned a remarkable $330 million in North America and $975 million globally by the end of its run. While “Oppenheimer” opened in second place behind “Barbie,” another box office behemoth, “The Odyssey” gets the satisfaction of debuting at No. 1 across the globe.

	“There’s a palpable excitement in theaters this weekend,” says Universal’s domestic distribution president Jim Orr. “Christopher Nolan is one of the most trusted filmmakers ever, and audiences know he’s going to deliver something that you want to experience on the big screen. We have showtimes in certain formats that are sold out for next weekend, the weekend after, and even mid-week.”

	Nolan has become synonymous with premium large formats, and “The Odyssey” is notable as the first feature shot entirely with Imax cameras. It’s a feat that Nolan has called his “longest-held ambition.” Audiences have taken note of the hype around these screens. Premium large formats contributed a massive 53% of the film’s domestic tally, with Imax accounting for $51.8 million or 23% of global ticket sales. Imax, which is dedicating its entire global footprint to “The Odyssey” for three weeks, has been scheduling showtimes around the clock in the coveted 70mm auditoriums, with some determined cinephiles enduring 2 a.m. and 7 a.m. screenings of the nearly three-hour epic.
	

	“’The Odyssey’ is among a rarified group of instant classic blockbusters in Imax history that fully leverage our platform; as bullish as we are on its success, our experience tells us that even we don’t know how this movie will grow our brand and business,” says Rich Gelfond, CEO of Imax. “With record pre-sales, critical and audience acclaim, and the way the film presents extraordinarily in Imax, we believe all the elements are in place for ‘The Odyssey’ to enjoy a very long and successful run.”

	Thanks to “The Odyssey,” the box office is rebounding after three consecutive duds: the Warner Bros. and DC adaptation “Supergirl,” Universal and Illumination sequel “Minions and Monsters” and Disney’s live action “Moana.” Despite those stumbles, domestic ticket sales are roughly 10% above 2025, according to Rentrak, and the summer season is expected to surpass $4 billion for the second time since the pandemic upended the industry.

	“Nolan’s latest epic has arrived at the perfect time, reminding everyone of the undeniable allure of the big-screen moviegoing experience,” says Paul Dergarabedian, Rentrak’s head of marketplace trends.

	“Moana” landed in a very distant second place with $19 million in its sophomore frame — a 56% decline from its disappointing $43 million debut. So far, Disney’s live-action remake has generated $82 million domestically and $178 million globally. Since “Moana” was so expensive (it carries a $250 million price tag) and theater owners get to keep roughly half of revenues, the film is primed to lose at least $100 million in its theatrical run. Disney’s summer has been mixed, with the studio fielding outsized hits like “The Devil Wears Prada 2” ($688 million) and “Toy Story 5” along with big-budget misfires like “Moana” and “Star Wars: The Mandalorian and Grogu” ($340 million against a $165 million budget).

	“Minions and Monsters” tied with “Toy Story 5” for third place, with each film bringing in $14.8 million over the weekend. 

	For “Minions and Monsters,” those ticket sales represent a 34% drop from the prior weekend and boost revenues to $137 million domestically and $323 million globally. Since prior entries in Universal and Illumination’s “Despicable Me” franchise have reached or surpassed the billion-dollar mark with ease, the newest “Minions” may wind up as the lowest-grossing of the bunch. However, this installment was produced for $85 million and will turn a profit for the studio.
	

	“Toy Story 5” is one summer’s biggest hits with $430 million in North America and $958 million worldwide after five weekends of release. It’ll soon surpass $1 billion and eventually overtake its predecessor, 2019’s “Toy Story 4” ($1.07 billion), as the highest-grossing entry in the beloved Pixar franchise.

	Meanwhile “Supergirl” dropped to No. 9 with $1.5 million in its fourth weekend. After a month in theaters, the latest comic book adaptation from Warner Bros. and DC is fizzling with $69.9 million domestically and $120 million globally. Given its hefty $170 million budget, “Supergirl” is projected to lose more than $120 million in its theatrical run. It’s the second notable flop in 2026 for Warner Bros. following this spring’s “The Bride!” which flatlined with $23 million worldwide against a $90 million budget.

	Elsewhere, A24’s comedy “The Invite” has collected $3.9 million over the weekend from 2,110 screens, its widest footprint to date. After about a month in limited release, Olivia Wilde’s movie has grossed a promising $15 million.

schema.headline: Box Office: ‘The Odyssey’ Stuns With $124 Million Opening Weekend, Third-Biggest Debut of the Year

title: FIFA World Cup Final 2026 Livestream: Here's Where to Watch the Spain vs. Argentina Online for Free
description:
If you purchase an independently reviewed product or service through a link on our website, Variety may receive an affiliate commission.

	After a m
body:
If you purchase an independently reviewed product or service through a link on our website, Variety may receive an affiliate commission.

	After a month of gameplay, the 2026 FIFA World Cup Final starts on Sunday, July 19. For the 23rd edition of the international soccer tournament final, the match takes place at New York New Jersey Stadium (MetLife Stadium) in East Rutherford, New Jersey. Kick off is at 3 p.m. ET/12 p.m. PT.

	If you want to catch the final live, the match broadcasts on FOX in English, as well as Telemundo in Spanish. It also livestreams on FOX One and Peacock, respectively.


	The World Cup closing ceremony starts at 1:30 p.m. ET/11:30 a.m. PT before the match begins. It features performances from Nicole Scherzinger, Laura Pausini and Robbie Williams, while it also has influencer IShowSpeed and actor Tom Cruise making special appearances.

	And ahead of Spain vs. Argentina, Jennifer Hudson performs the U.S. national anthem, “The Star-Spangled Banner.”

	Additionally, Madonna, Shakira, BTS and Justin Bieber are scheduled to perform with an approximate start time of 4 p.m. ET/1 p.m. PT. Meanwhile, Jennifer Hudson is set to perform “The Star-Spangled Banner” before kickoff.

	The World Cup Final may be a draw to a number of celebrity soccer fans, such as Javier Bardem and Penélope Cruz, Antonio Banderas, Rafael Nadal and others, as well as Leonardo DiCaprio, Owen Wilson, Tom Holland, Selena Gomez, Drake and more. Tune-in to see which celebrities are attending the final match.

	And with so much star power on and off the pitch, the World Cup Final is expected to be a ratings win for all networks that are broadcasting and live-streaming the match.

	Want to watch FOX, or Telemundo online for free? You can get access to these networks through streaming platforms, like DirecTV, Fubo, Hulu + Live TV, FOX One and others. In fact, some of these streamers offer free trials too.



						
			
	

					DirecTV
	
					

	DirecTV is offering a five-day free trial, which will let you watch FOX, as well as Telemundo for free. There’s an app available that comes with key features, including unlimited DVR storage and up to three devices to stream. In addition, you’ll get access to local and cable channels.

	For FOX and Telemundo, you can sign up for DirecTV’s “Entertainment” Signature Package for $59.99 for the first month of service ($89.99/month afterward) and get more than 90 channels.


						
			
	

					Fox Corp.
	
					

	FOX has its own streaming service called FOX One, which features every network under the FOX umbrella, including FOX News, FOX Weather, FOX Nation, FOX Business, FOX Sports, FS2, Big Ten Network, FOX Deportes, FOX Network and your local FOX station. FOX One starts at $19.99/month ($199.99/year), or you can signup with the service as a channel add-on for Prime Video. In fact, go with Prime Video and get a three-day free trial just for signing up.

	Please note: You have to be a subscriber to Prime Video to get FOX One as an add-on channel.

	Sign up for a 30-day free trial to take advantage of all that Amazon Prime has to offer, including access to Prime Video, Prime Gaming and Amazon Photos; fast free shipping in less than two days with Prime Delivery; in-store discounts at Whole Foods Market; access to exclusive shopping deals and events — such as Black Friday in November — and much more. Learn about Amazon Prime and its benefits here.


						
			
	

					NBCUniversal
	
					

	The World Cup Final livestream on Telemundo is accessible on Peacock, so the best way to watch the tournament in Spanish is to get a subscription to the streaming services at $10.99/month.

	Not a subscriber? You can get a Peacock monthly subscription to stream the World Cup Final online, and keep it to watch other hit movies and TV shows — including “Wicked: For Good,” “Hamnet,” “Kill Bill: The Whole Bloody Affair,” “Oppenheimer,” “The Traitors,” “Vanderpump Rules” and others.

	Luckily, the 23rd edition of the World Cup Final is not a pay-per-view event, so there is no PPV price needed. You can watch the Spanish-language broadcast online simply with a Peacock subscription.


						
			
	

					Sling TV
	
					

	Sling is one of the best options for affordable live streaming with the Sling Blue package, which goes for $45.99/month. The package includes FOX. World Cup fans can broadcast the match in Spanish on Telemundo, which is featured in the Sling Latino package for just $10/month.


	Please note: Pricing and channel availability are subject to your local TV market.


						
			
	

					Fubo
	
					

	Fubo offers a five-day free trial for new users who sign up, and the streaming service includes access to FOX, as well as Telemundo. The service offers a promo that’ll get you up to $30 off the first month at $63.99 (reg. $88.99/month) for the Fubo Pro plan. Once set up, customers get access to the FOX One streaming service (via Fubo login credentials) and more than 220 live TV channels too.


						
			
	

					Hulu
	
					

	Hulu + Live TV, which has a three-day free trial, gives you access to FOX and Telemundo, along with more than 100 live TV channels for $89.99/month. The streaming service is bundled with an expanded content library with Disney+, Hulu and ESPN Unlimited at no additional cost.

schema.headline: FIFA World Cup Final 2026 Livestream: Here's Where to Watch the Spain vs. Argentina Online for Free

title: ‘The End of Oak Street’ Preview: Anne Hathaway and Ewan McGregor Try Not to Get Eaten Alive by Dinosaurs in 14 Minutes of 4DX Footage
description:
What’s more thrilling than watching dinosaurs try to eat people alive on the big screen?

	Watching dinosaurs try to eat people alive on the big scr
body:
What’s more thrilling than watching dinosaurs try to eat people alive on the big screen?

	Watching dinosaurs try to eat people alive on the big screen in 4DX, as unveiled in a special preview of “The End of Oak Street” on Saturday night in Los Angeles. 

	In the sci-fi movie from “It Follows” director David Robert Mitchell and producer J.J Abrams (working under his Bad Robot banner), married couple Denise (Anne Hathaway) and Greg (Ewan McGregor) must fight to save their family once a cosmic event rips through Oak Street, relocating their home from their familiar, ordinary neighborhood.
	

	A crowd of journalists and influencers previewed 14 minutes of new footage, presented in 4DK: Denise and Greg exit their house in a desperate search for their dog, Starbuck (an affectionate nod to “Battlestar Galactica”), leaving their two kids behind, played by “My Old Ass” breakout Maisy Stella and “Sweet Tooth” lead Christian Convery. But a baseball bat and shotgun as weapons can only do so much as Denise and Greg split up, travel across their vast suburban neighborhood, narrowly cheat death from a speedy dinosaur (as shown in the original trailer) and attempt to escape in their family car — only to discover that not only has Oak Street been mysteriously relocated, but they have been plopped right into the world of roaming dinosaurs, in all different sizes and terrifying types.


	It’s safe to say there’s no easy escape from Oak Street.

	The audience giggled nervously as their seats rocked and rolled during the tense chase scene, and whenever the dinosaur went in for an attack, a squirt of water shot out from behind each audience member’s seat. Mitchell entered the theater just after the clip ended and was delighted by the immersive effects. “Especially seeing the seats shifting with the driving!” he remarked, laughing, too.
	

	Mitchell — who stopped by while on a break from production on the highly anticipated “They Follow” —  told the crowd of journalists and influencers how he imagined plopping dinosaurs into suburban life: “The idea of a normal neighborhood was something that is so fantastical. I thought the merging of those two things would be interesting, grounding the spectacle.”

	Beyond being advertised as an end-of-summer blockbuster, Mitchell and Abrams emphasized that “The End of Oak Street” is anchored by the core family dynamics. “The brilliance of what you wrote is that each of the characters has a secret, essentially. Each of the characters has something they’re wrestling with, but they’re a family that you so connect with and so root for and believe in,” Abrams said. “It’s not a sadistic movie. It’s got a big heart at its core.”

	Mitchell explained that he needed a cast “that brought a certain level of performance and naturalism to the family so that we could really be drawn in.” He said, “They are us. It’s the idea of being able to view ourselves. They are a traditional family.”

	For example, Hathaway’s Denise was written as a loving mother with her own ambitions. “There’s this conflict of, ‘Where am I in my life and how am I connecting to the people I care about?'” he said, adding, “Each of the kids has their own issues. They are all put into conflict, and those issues are elevated when they find themselves in this incredibly dangerous situation.”

	Mitchell went on to explain how he switches perspective between Hathaway and McGregor’s characters as they discover the horrors spread across their neighborhood, including one moment when an older neighbor is found dead on their floor near a trail of blood. “It’s really about the subjective and the objective, finding ways to express a moment, visually, in an interesting way,” he said.

	Michael Giacchino, who scored all three “Jurassic World” films and “The Lost World” video game, brings a tense, eerie atmosphere through the music heard in the footage. “He identifies, like any great composer, the heartbeat of the scene, something you might have been aware of,” Abrams said. “He will excavate something, emphasize something, and all of a sudden, everything gets elevated because of it.”

	Abrams compared “The End of Oak Street” to a great episode of “The Twilight Zone.”
	

	“On one hand, you sort of know what you’re going to get, but you really don’t know what you’re going to get,” he explained. “The story is so specific and unique to these characters and so emotional. When I go to the movies, my dream is to just be taken and to experience a wealth of emotions and things, and get to have a really full meal. It’s rare to do that in a movie that is a genre film.”

	“The End of Oak Street” will roar into theaters Aug. 14 from Warner Bros. Pictures. In addition to being shown in select 4DX theaters nationwide, the Warner Bros film will be shown in Imax.

schema.headline: ‘The End of Oak Street’ Preview: Anne Hathaway and Ewan McGregor Try Not to Get Eaten Alive by Dinosaurs in 14 Minutes of 4DX Footage

title: Jennifer Finch, L7 Bassist and Tireless Indie-Rock Champion, Dies After Battle With Brain Cancer
description: Jennifer Finch, bassist, singer and songwriter for the veteran indie-rock band L7, died Saturday after a battle with an aggressive form of brain ca
body:
Jennifer Finch, bassist, singer and songwriter for the veteran indie-rock band L7, died Saturday after a battle with an aggressive form of brain cancer, according to a statement from the band’s rep. She was 59.
	

	A popular and well-known presence on the indie-rock scene of the 1990s, earlier in her career Finch played with Courtney Love in a band called Sugar Baby Doll and was an avid photographer and writer. In just one testament to the esteem in which she was held, a GoFundMe announced last week to help with her treatment quickly passed its $350,000 goal and received contributions from members of Green Day, Pearl Jam, Tool and many others.


	“We are shattered by the loss of our beloved bandmate, sister and friend Jennifer Finch, whose fierce spirit, humor and boundless creativity helped shape L7 and changed all of our lives forever,” the band wrote on social media. “Jennifer was a true original who lived entirely on her own terms, and the impact she made on music, art and everyone lucky enough to know her cannot be measured. We love her beyond words and will carry her with us always. Rest in power our dear friend.


	“With Love, L7.”


	A Los Angeles native, Finch was born in 1966 and pursued photography and art as a teenager. She began taking photos of her friends on the L.A. punk scene of the early ‘80s, including members of Redd Kross, Bad Religion, the Cramps and others, which were displayed at the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame decades later.
	

	She took up the bass, relocated to San Francisco and joined Love in Sugar Baby Doll, which also featured future Babes in Toyland cofounder Kat Bjelland. She returned to L.A. and had a brief stint in the all-female band the Pandoras before joining L7 in 1986.

	The group’s debut album, produced by Bad Religion’s Brett Gurewitz and released on Epitaph, featured the band performing live in a blurry, proto-grunge photo on its cover and opened with a song called “Bite the Wax Tadpole,” a fitting statement of their sound and attitude. The group toured heavily with multiple bands of the alt-rock era, uniting with Sub Pop Records and veteran Seattle producer Jack Endino for their second album, 1990’s “Smell the Magic.”

	Yet it was the group’s third outing, “Bricks Are Heavy,” that made the most impact. Recorded late in 1991 with Nirvana/ Smashing Pumpkins producer Butch Vig, the album featured the group’s biggest hit, “Pretend We’re Dead,” and gave their gnarly sound a sharp power-pop crunch. It was released on Slash Records, which was riding high from the success of Faith No More and had a distribution pact with the powerful Warner Bros. Records. The band followed with “Hungry for Stink” in 1994 but Finch left the group two years later.

	Over the following years she joined the band OtherStarPeople, which released an album on Interscope in 1999, and later the punk band the Shocker. In 2014 Finch joined a reformed L7, releasing an album called “Scatter the Rats” via Joan Jett’s Blackheart Records in 2019, and continued to perform with them until this year. She announced her cancer diagnosis early in July and died just days later.

	While she leaves behind a solid discography, Finch’s influence was perhaps most pervasive on young musicians, particularly women. Molly Neuman, drummer for Riot Grrl act Bratmobile and currently president of the indie-centric publishing administrator Songtrust, wrote on social media: “I am so sorry you’re gone. An inspiration in everything I’ve ever done. I am grateful for the time I had with you and will do my best to honor your legacy every day.”

schema.headline: Jennifer Finch, L7 Bassist and Tireless Indie-Rock Champion, Dies After Battle With Brain Cancer

title: Andrew Tate and Brother Tristan Arrested in Miami
description: Manosphere influencers Andrew and Tristan Tate were arrested by federal officers Saturday afternoon in Miami, according to AP. The charges against t
body:
Manosphere influencers Andrew and Tristan Tate were arrested by federal officers Saturday afternoon in Miami, according to AP. The charges against the brothers were not immediately made public.

	This isn’t the first time the Tate brothers have found themselves in cuffs. Back in 2022, the controversial media figures were arrested in Romania by anti-terrorism authorities for human trafficking and rape charges. In 2023, Andrew and Tristan were officially charged with those offenses by the Romanian government, according to the BBC.
	

	Andrew Tate has continuously denied all wrongdoing. He told the media in 2023 after he was released from house arrest, “We’ve been completely innocent since the beginning of this, and I have to give absolute faith to the Romanian judicial system for finally making the right decision and letting us free.”


	According to Reuters, a Romanian court recently lifted all preventative judicial control measures against the Tates while authorities conduct further investigations into their charges.

	In May of 2025, the BBC reported that British prosecutors authorized 21 charges against Andrew and Tristian Tate, including rape, actual bodily harm and human trafficking. Andrew Tate faces 10 charges connected to three alleged victims, while Tristan Tate faces 11 charges tied to one alleged victim.

	Andrew Tate first came into the public eye as a guest on the U.K.’s “Big Brother” in 2016. He was later removed from the show after a video surfaced in which he appeared to be hitting a woman with a belt. Andrew Tate and the women later claimed the actions were consensual. Since then, he’s risen to internet stardom as a controversial, hyper-masculine influencer.

schema.headline: Andrew Tate and Brother Tristan Arrested in Miami

title: MIFFest Opens Ninth Edition in Kuala Lumpur, Earns ASEAN Records Recognition
description: The ninth Malaysia International Film Festival (MIFFest) opened on July 18 at MyTown Shopping Centre, kicking off an eight-day showcase of internati
body:
The ninth Malaysia International Film Festival (MIFFest) opened on July 18 at MyTown Shopping Centre, kicking off an eight-day showcase of international cinema and marking the festival’s official recognition from ASEAN Records.

	The opening ceremony drew filmmakers, industry leaders and stars to a red carpet ahead of the evening’s program. 

	Joanne Goh, founder and president of MIFFest, delivered the welcome address alongside this year’s festival ambassadors, Bront Palarae, Daiyan Trisha and Jack Tan. Goh outlined the inspiration behind the festival’s 2026 theme.
	

	“This year, our theme is Resonance. Resonance is the lasting echo of a story. It is the emotion that remains long after the credits roll, the conversations that continue beyond the cinema, and the connections that film creates across cultures, generations, and borders,” Goh said.


	Datuk Manoharan Periasamy, chair of Tourism Malaysia, addressed the audience, reaffirming support for MIFFest’s role in strengthening the country’s creative industry and cultural diplomacy. Datuk Bahria Mohd Tamil, deputy secretary general (strategic communications and creative industry) at the Ministry of Communications, and Datuk Azmir Saifuddin Mutalib, CEO of the National Film Development Corporation Malaysia (FINAS), also attended. Goh presented tokens of appreciation to the festival ambassadors during the ceremony.

	The evening’s centerpiece was the presentation of the ASEAN Records title to MIFFest, recognizing the festival’s ASEAN On Screen platform and its Malaysia Golden Global Awards (MGGA) program as a lasting hub for Southeast Asian cinema. Goh accepted the recognition on behalf of the festival.
	

	“When we introduced ASEAN On Screen, our vision was to create a lasting platform where the stories of Southeast Asia could be celebrated, discovered, and shared with the world. Through cinema, we hope to continue connecting our diverse voices with international audiences and inspiring future generations of storytellers,” Goh said.

	ASEAN On Screen launched in 2025 during Malaysia’s ASEAN chairship and has since become a permanent annual program at MIFFest. The ceremony also recognized the festival’s preliminary selection committee and the jury panels for the semi-final and final stages.

	Following the ceremony, guests moved to GSC MyTown for the world premiere of the festival’s opening film, “Baga: Tomorrow Belongs To No One,” directed by Ariff Zulkarnain. The film unfolds in a devout fishing community on Malaysia’s East Coast, following two young lovers, Jani and Melur, whose plans for the future are upended by an unplanned pregnancy, poverty and addiction, pushing Jani toward the drug trade. Zulkarnain drew on his own upbringing for the story.

	Leading the delegation for this year’s opening film were executive producer Shafiq Yusof, director Zulkarnain and Malaysian actor Syafiq Kyle, alongside Cambodian actress Sarita Reth. Also in attendance were Jojo Goh, actor-director Nam Ron and actress June Lojong, among other guests from Malaysia and abroad.

	This year’s program also includes Russian Film Week, presented with the Ministry of Culture of the Russian Federation, Roskino and Mosfilm, offering free screenings of four Russian films. Additional strands include ASEAN On Screen, Hong Kong Cinema in Flux, New Rhythms of Indian Cinema, Open Air Cinema, masterclasses and industry forums.

	The festival runs through July 25.

schema.headline: MIFFest Opens Ninth Edition in Kuala Lumpur, Earns ASEAN Records Recognition

title: Brazil’s Gramado Fest Set to Honor ‘I’m Still Here’ Star Selton Mello and ‘The Secret Agent’ Standout Maria Fernanda Cândido
description: As Brazil’s explosion on the Oscar and A-Fest scene turns its top actors into international stars, two of its biggest breakouts, “I’m Not Here” co-l
body:
As Brazil’s explosion on the Oscar and A-Fest scene turns its top actors into international stars, two of its biggest breakouts, “I’m Not Here” co-lead Selton Mello and “The Secret Agent” star María Fernanda Cândido, will be honored at Brazil’s Gramado Film Festival, bringing the aura of burgeoning international renown to one of the country’s oldest and biggest film events.

	Taking place in a mountain resort famed for its Alpine-style architecture, snow, chocolate shops and fondue restaurants, the 54th edition of Brazil’s Gramado Film Festival will fittingly open with a world premiere that again contrasts with the country’s tropical image: “Antártida” (“Antarctic”), set at a South Pole base.  
	

	Produced by Brazilian media giant Globo, “Antártida” is helmed by Bruno Safadi, a director on breakout TV series “Aruanas.” It is penned by Claudia Jovin, one of Brazil’s hottest up-and-coming scribes after lead writing Globoplay 2025 smash hit “Perfect Days.”   

	Six Brazilian features will compete in Gramado for the main Kikito prize, five of them world premieres, also taking in “Chorão: Só os Loucos Sabem,” “Justino – Nos Bastidores do Reino,” “Leite em Pó,” “Nosso Segredo” and “Pele de Rinoceronte.” The sixth, “Gugu’s World” (“Feito Pipa”), won best film at Berlin’s Generation KPlus this year.

	Running Aug. 12-22, Gramado will close with Chilean Dominga Sotomayor’s “La Perra,” well received at Cannes’ Directors’ Fortnight and co-produced by top Brazilian company RT Features. 
	

	Four features vie for Gramado’s documentary Kikito: “Afrontosa,” by Coraci Ruiz and Julio Matos; “Empeleitada,” from Micaele Xukuru, Chico Ludermir and Sergio Borges; “Gonzaguinha, da Maior Liberdade,” directed by Susanna Lira; and “O Projeto,” helmed by Sabrina Fidalgo and Yvan Rodic.

	Gramado will also stage the awaited Brazilian premiere “Emergência 53” Episode 1, a Conspiração production for Globoplay from two driving forces of Globo’s small hit “Under Pressure.“ Andrucha Waddington and Cláudio Torres. At a time of gathering TV artistic caution stood out at this year’s Berlinale Series Market for its boldness, the mobile emergency medical unit procedural winning the event’s first Studio Babelsberg Production Excellence Award for outstanding creative projects that combine artistic ambition with international potential.



	

					‘Emergency 53’  Courtesy of Globoplay
	



	Actresses Ana Flavia Cavalcanti and Camila Morgado and film critic Marcos Santuario served as curators of this year’s fest’s selection.

	A Globo telenovela child star who went on to headline multiple Brazilian movies, Mello played the role of father Rubens Paiva in “I’m Still Here,” the first Portuguese-language Brazilian feature to win an Academy Award, in addition to to making a splash on Brazil’s arthouse scene directing “The Clown” and “The Movie of My Life.” 

	Cândido is known internationally for her roles in Marco Bellocchio’s “The Traitor” “Fantastic Beasts: The Secrets of Dumbledore” and “The Secret Agent,” which scored four Oscar-nominations, an all-time record for Brazil.

	President of the Brazilian Film Academy, producer Renata Almeida Magalhães (“Favela Gay”) will receive the Eduardo Abelin Trophy. Andrea Beltrão, nominated for an Intl. Emmy Award for her performance in “Hebe,” will take home the Oscarito Trophy. Actor, screenwriter and director Marcos Caruso (“Brazil Avenue”) will be honored with the City of Gramado Trophy.

	The Gramado Film Festival is organized by Gramadotur, the tourism and culture authority of Gramado Town Hall. The 40,000-inhabitant mountain resort town liesin Rio Grande do Sul, Brazil’s southernmost state. It has a distinctly Bavarian feel, reflecting the influx of German settlers in the 19th century.
	

	Rosa Helena Volk, president of Gramadotur, stressed the fact that all feature films in the competitive sections this year will offer audio description and Brazilian Sign Language interpretation and subtitles.

	A closer look at some of the festival’s major films:

	Opening Film

	“Antártida” stars Andrea Beltrão, Marina Ruy Barbosa, Leandra Leal, Antonio Calloni, Lázaro Ramos and João Vitor Silva. The pic was lensed in Globo’s modern studios in Rio, with technology that simulated sequences in Antarctica. It turns on Inês, who is assaultedat a Brazilian South Pole base with scientists and military personnel, turning all the men into suspects. Deputy commander Elisabeth leads an investigation to find the perpetrator. Paris Filmes will release “Antártida” in local theaters on Sept. 17.

	“I come from auteur cinema, but I had the opportunity to make a major production with all the resources and time we needed,” Safadi said in the ceremony for the fest’s lineup announcement.

	“It took me nine years to see this project come to life. What was missing was the technology. I can’t wait to feel the warmth of the audience,” said Jouvin.



	

					Marina Ruy Barbosa in ‘Antártida’  Crédit Fabio Rocha
									Fabio Rocha/TVGlobo
	



	Main Competition Films:

	“Chorão: Só os Loucos Sabem” (Hugo Prata and Felipe Novaes) depicts the life of Chorão, the frontman of local pop group Charlie Brown Jr. José Loreto and Nanda Marques star in the pic that is produced by Bravura Cinematográfica in co-production with Globo Filmes.

	“Justino – Nos Bastidores do Reino” (José Eduardo Belmonte). Set in 1980s Rio de Janeiro in the 1980s and produced by Mercado Filmes. Vet Belmonte helms the film that stars Christian Malheiros, Antonio Pitanga and Caio Blat. In the story, the protagonist finds in a powerful neo-Pentecostal church an opportunity to escape poverty.

	“Leite em Pó” (Carlos Segundo). Produced by O Sopro do Tempo, Vitrine Filmes and Les Valseurs, the story of a rock-obsessed musician whose life falls apart after family loss. The cast includes Vinicius de Oliveira, Antonio Pitanga, Zezita Matos and Rejane Faria.

	“Nosso Segredo” (Grace Passô). Produced by Entre Filmes, co-production by Globo Filmes, the story of a family’s attempt to rebuild its routine following a recent loss. The movie stars Robert Frank, Efraim Santos, Jéssica Gaspar, Flip and Ju Colombo.
	

	“Pele de Rinoceronte” (Marcello Ludwig Maia). 1970s-set and starring Débora Falabella as a reporter for a popular newspaper whose life changes following a murder that shocked Brazil. The drama also features Naruna Costa and Irandhir Santos. República Pureza produced the pic, in co-production with Canal Brasil and Telecine. “‘Pele de Rinoceronte’ is a deeply personal story that addresses what I see as a major issue in Brazil: violence against women,” Maia said.

	“Gugu’s World” (“Feito Pipa,”Allan Deberton) centers one a boy who dreams of becoming a great soccer player. Raised by his grandmother, he fears having to move back in with his father. Starring Lázaro Ramos, Yuri Gomes and Teca Pereira and produced by Deberton Filmes and Biônica Filmes (“Charcoal,” “Toll”).



	

					Rosa Pereira Helena Volk, presidente of Gramadotur  Credit: Edison Vara/Ag.Pressphoto
									DIEGO VARA

schema.headline: Brazil’s Gramado Fest Set to Honor ‘I’m Still Here’ Star Selton Mello and ‘The Secret Agent’ Standout Maria Fernanda Cândido

title: ‘Mexodus’ Review: The Underground Railroad Runs South to Mexico in a Pasadena Playhouse Musical That Feels as Spirited as a Two-Man ‘Hamilton’
description: To paraphrase Aaliyah: Cast size ain’t nothing but a number. In the realm of the theater, no one exactly equates the number of headshots on a Playbi
body:
To paraphrase Aaliyah: Cast size ain’t nothing but a number. In the realm of the theater, no one exactly equates the number of headshots on a Playbill page with the ultimate fulfillment a stage show provides. Still, a question may arise: How few people can you have in a musical and still generate just as many killowatt-hours of energy as a full ensemble production?

	It’s not a theoretical question. An answer strongly comes to us with the musical now playing at Pasadena Playhouse, “Mexodus,” a two-hander that can feel like a 20-hander as you’re going with the boisterous flow and forgetting to do the math. The show has Brian Quijada and Nygel D. Robinson as both its writers and its stars, and these are two fellows who ought to be able to write their own tickets for some time to come, on the basis of this (asterisk: at least to the extent that anyone in the theater can), as actors and/or songwriters. Heck, if either of them decided to go really minimalist after this and do a one-man show, I’d be among the first in line.
	

	But in “Mexodus,” it takes two to tango, or to collaborate on a mixture of musical styles, with the complementary flavors of hip-hop and traditional Tex-Mex balladry at the top of the list. It very much feels like a spawn of “Hamilton,” in that it’s a period piece set in a century well before ours with a considerable amount of rapping at the outset. That’s an anachronism you may enjoy or even feel exhilarated by, even as you’re hoping maybe not the entirety of the show plays out in that style. It doesn’t, of course. Part of what gives “Mexodus” such a kick is how expert Quijada and Robinson turn out to be as writers and singers in a surprising number of genres that expand throughout the show in a kind of beautifully inverse proportion to the number of actual players on stage.
	

	Two things to know straight off: One, “Mexodus” is a good time at the theater. And, two, it’s a slavery story. If those two key factors seem like they might cancel each other out, you won’t be the first to wonder how a narrative spun off from the United States’ greatest shame, set in the days before emancipation, can be reconciled with feel-good entertainment. There’s an easy answer to that: Most of the action takes place south of the border, after the enslaved Henry (Robinson) has made his escape to Mexico, where he finds an uneasy benefactor in a rancher, Carlos (Quijada). The horrors of what Henry has left behind — and could easily be returned to — are hardly skipped over. But ultimately it’s a story about the sometimes tentative, sometimes tight relationship between Black and brown people… ostensibly in the 1860s, but by historical extension the 2020s, too.

	The question is set up: Can Latinos and Blacks form a more perfect union as they both deal, with varying degrees of deadliness, with white America? In exploring that coming together of two marginalized (to say the least) North American cultures, “Mexodus” finally lands in a place of not just cautious optimism but a good reason to throw a musical-theater party.

	Before the narrative starts up in earnest, the show opens with a good stretch of fourth-wall-breaking, as Quijada and Robinson greet the house and explain the rules of how all the music will be created in the intermission-less hour-and-a-half to follow. A full vocal and instrumental sound will be put together via looping, which will not require much explanation for anyone who knows anything about, say, Ed Sheeran’s live performances. (Ariana Grande even has a looping exercise in her current tour.) For a less pop-savvy theater crowd that might require more backstory on the gambit, it involves Robinson and/or Quijada singing a background part, or playing a drumbeat or acoustic guitar riff, then having these bits lap and layer over one another, with the use of a turntable or pedal or knob or offstage assistant. There’s an impressive magic effect that occurs when one or two men are able to quickly turn themselves into a pit band, or off-Broadway chorale. But to the duo’s credit, there’s at least as much kismet happening when they aren’t messing with these effects at all but knocking us out with, say, an unexpected Spanish-guitar duet. There’s probably a version of this show that these two could pull off without any of the looping tech; it’d be interesting to see and hear them try doing a “Mexodus Unplugged.” But probably no one in the audience will begrudge their ability to come up with a full sound and heavy pulse by the time a mirror ball lights up at stage right.
	

	“Mexodus” does mean to be a history lesson, without becoming too pedantic a peda. In their narrator mode, Quijada and Robinson offer a stat that an estimated 4,000 to 10,000 enslaved people made their way to freedom on a lesser-known Underground Railroad that went south instead of north. Once the story gets underway, they don’t break in with too many more factoids. But each of the two principals does get a sort of time-out in the action in which they recount what seems to be an actually autobiographical memory of interaction earlier in their lives with “the other” — not the white other, but with Blacks, in Quijada’s case, and Latinos, in Robinson’s. It’s easy to imagine a director less sensitive than David Mendizábal trying to convince the writer-actors that the show doesn’t need these twin outlier moments. But these anecdotes serve as lovely grace notes in reminding the audience that the relatively happy outcome to the fictional-historical story isn’t meant to suggest that the Black and brown populi have exactly been in perfect alignment since the mid-1860s. Robinson and Quijada make such a perfect dream team, you want to believe that everyone they’re standing in for in real life is as sympatico as their characters turn out to be. These reflective, personal bits help ground the show in the inevitable realization that things are tense all over… still.

	But you come to “Mexodus” to be elevated, not to keep crashing back to earth. It’s a show where issues of melanin meet melatonin, and if that sounds at all like a forced marriage, you haven’t seen the ease of how Quijada and Robinson’s writing and performance styles are wed here.



	

					Brian Quijada and Nygel D. Robinson in “Mexodus” at Pasadena Playhouse
									Thomas Mundell
	



	It may help that L.A. is getting this show about as fresh out of New York’s heat and humidity as theatrical productions come. Quijada and Robinson initially performed it in a twice-extended run at the Minetta Lane Theatre in New York in 2025, then quickly revived it for an additional engagement at the Daryl Roth Theatre that wrapped up on June 14 — with NYC being enraptured enough to give the show four Lucille Lortel Awards, four Outer Critics Circle Awards, three Drama Desk Awards, Off Broadway Alliance Award and a Drama League Award. After all that, they barely had time to take a cross-country flight before picking things up in Pasadena, whose Playhouse barely had time to usher out its acclaimed “Brigadoon” revival in time to make way for this two-man tuner. It feels like Pasadena just got in a big shipment of adrenaline, in other words.
	

	And almost regardless of the merits of the show itself (but not quite), it’s worth catching even if you’re just a fan of hungry and talented actors going above and beyond in making work — ultra-high-quality work — for themselves. Now, here are two guys who know how to build, if not quite an actual Underground Railroad, one hell of a funicular.

	“Mexodus” continues at Pasadena Playhouse through Aug. 2. Ticket information can be found at PasadenaPlayhouse.org.

schema.headline: ‘Mexodus’ Review: The Underground Railroad Runs South to Mexico in a Pasadena Playhouse Musical That Feels as Spirited as a Two-Man ‘Hamilton’

title: How to Watch the World Cup Final Halftime Show Live Online
description:
If you purchase an independently reviewed product or service through a link on our website, Variety may receive an affiliate commission.

	The FIFA
body:
If you purchase an independently reviewed product or service through a link on our website, Variety may receive an affiliate commission.

	The FIFA World Cup is getting its own Super Bowl-style halftime show for the first time.

	Madonna, Shakira, Justin Bieber and BTS will co-headline the inaugural FIFA World Cup Final Halftime Show on Sunday, July 19, during the championship match between Spain and Argentina. Burna Boy, conductor Gustavo Dudamel and New York’s PS 22 Chorus featuring Coldplay will also take part in the performance, which was curated by Coldplay frontman Chris Martin.
	

	The easiest way to watch the World Cup final halftime show online in the U.S. is through a live TV streaming service that carries Fox, which will broadcast the match in English.


	Cord-cutters can stream Fox through platforms such as DirecTV, Fubo and Hulu + Live TV.

	For Spanish-language coverage, the match and halftime show will air on Telemundo and stream live on Peacock, which holds the tournament’s Spanish-language streaming rights in the U.S.

	The World Cup final kicks off at 3 p.m. ET from New York New Jersey Stadium — the FIFA-designated name for MetLife Stadium in East Rutherford, N.J. — meaning the halftime show will likely begin sometime after 4 p.m., depending on stoppage time and the pace of the match.

	The show is being produced in partnership with Global Citizen and will support the FIFA Global Citizen Education Fund, an initiative seeking to raise $100 million to expand access to education and soccer programs for children around the world.

	The performance will take place during the final between defending champion Argentina and Spain. Argentina advanced after defeating England 2-1 in the semifinals, while Spain secured its place with a 2-0 victory over France.
	

	Stream the World Cup halftime show live online here.

schema.headline: How to Watch the World Cup Final Halftime Show Live Online

title: 20 Best Sword-and-Sandal Films, From ‘The Odyssey’ to ‘Gladiator’
description:
Jul 19, 2026 8:00am PT	
	

			


			
			
	

									Everett/Universal Pictures
	

		
	
				

	It’s been decades since cinemagoers ha
body:
Jul 19, 2026 8:00am PT	
	

			


			
			
	

									Everett/Universal Pictures
	

		
	
				

	It’s been decades since cinemagoers have been treated to a high quality sword-and-sandal film, so leave it to the great Christopher Nolan to reinvigorate the dying genre with his thrilling take on Homer’s classic “The Odyssey.” Needless to say, Elon Musk and his army of trolls couldn’t have been more wrong about this one.

	The first film to be shot entirely in Imax 70mm, Nolan’s latest tells the tale of Odysseus (Matt Damon), who embarks on an epic decade-long quest home to Ithaca — encountering goddesses, armored giants, cyclops, Scylla and sirens along the way — after guiding the Greeks to victory in the decade-long Trojan War. Back on Ithaca, his grieving wife Penelope (Anne Hathaway) and callow son Telemachus (Tom Holland) ward off dozens of suitors feasting the family out of house and home as they vie for her hand in marriage (and Odysseus’ throne). 

	Nolan’s “The Odyssey” is sure to be recognized in a big way come Oscar time, but where does it rank among the greatest sword-and-sandal films ever?


			
			
	Troy (2004)
			
										
					Image Credit: Everett				
					
				
	Christopher Nolan was all set to replace Wolfgang Petersen as director of this adaptation of Homer’s “Iliad,” from a screenplay by David Benioff, but when Petersen’s “Batman vs. Superman” project blew up, Petersen went back to “Troy” and Warner Bros. offered Nolan “Batman Begins” as consolation. Anyway, Benioff’s screenplay completely bastardizes Homer’s tale, even killing off Agamemnon (!), though the Achilles/Hector showdown is thrilling and the casting is pretty spot-on, including Brad Pitt as Achilles, Eric Bana as Hector, Sean Bean as Odysseus and Bryan Cox as Agamemnon. At least it planted the seed for Benioff and Bean’s reunion on “Game of Thrones.”
		
			
			
	Noah (2014)
			
										
					Image Credit: Everett				
					
				
	Darren Aronofsky’s biblical epic retelling the story of Noah’s Ark caused quite the stir, with the filmmaker warring with Paramount over final cut and the company adding a disclaimer to the film’s promos to appease Christian groups since it departs from the source material. Plus, it was banned in China and a number of majority-Muslim countries for religious reasons. Despite all the hullabaloo, Aronofsky’s film is beautifully shot by acclaimed DP Matthew Libatique (“Black Swan,” “A Star Is Born”), boasts a powerful lead performance from Russell Crowe in the title role and promotes an urgent environmentalist message.
		
			
			
	Caligula (1980)
			
										
					Image Credit: Everett				
					
				
	Helen Mirren, who plays the courtesan turned wife to deviant Roman Emperor Caligula, once described Tinto Brass’ sex-and-sandal period farce as an “irresistible mix of art and genitals,” and far be it from me to question the queen. Brass infamously had the film ripped away from him and recut by producer Bob Guccione, founder of Penthouse, who edited out much of the comedy/politics and added in a number of hardcore pornographic sex scenes. As it stands, this portrait of the third Roman emperor’s brief reign, written by Gore Vidal, has reached cult-classic status for its amusing medley of sex, Roman politics and an all-star cast of British thespians — Mirren, Malcolm McDowell, Peter O’Toole and John Gielgud among them — hamming it up.
		
			
			
	Conan the Barbarian (1982)
			
										
					Image Credit: Everett				
					
				
	From acclaimed screenwriter John Milius, the man who not only co-wrote “Apocalypse Now” but performed rewrites on “Dirty Harry” and “Jaws,” comes this fantasy extravaganza about the barbarian son of a blacksmith, Conan (Arnold Schwarzenegger), who is out for revenge against the evil cult leader Thulsa Doom (James Earl Jones) after he murders his parents. It is, more than anything, a fun film for kids with a game cast clad in eye-catching costumes that served as a coming-out party for former bodybuilder Schwarzenegger, who proved a capable and convincing leading man.
		
			
			
	The 7th Voyage of Sinbad (1958)
			
										
					Image Credit: Everett				
					
				
	Nathan Juran’s 1958 swashbuckling fantasy is the first of three films chronicling the adventures of Sinbad, heir to the Caliph of Baghdad, who sails the high seas and crosses paths with cyclops, a Medusa and, in a memorable climactic showdown, a skeleton warrior, all rendered via eye-catching stop-motion animation. On top of the fun special effects and action sequences, “The 7th Voyage of Sinbad” also features an outstanding score from legendary composer Bernard Herrmann, who also crafted the scores to “Citizen Kane,” “Vertigo,” “Psycho,” “Taxi Driver,” and a number of other cinema classics.
		
			
			
	A Funny Thing Happened on the Way to the Forum (1966)
			
										
					Image Credit: Everett				
					
				
	From celebrated British comedy filmmaker Richard Lester, who helmed the Beatles’ films “A Hard Day’s Night” and “Help!”, comes this musical-comedy inspired by the work of Roman playwright Plautus and adapted from the Sondheim stage musical. It stars Zero Mostel as Pseudolus, a slave during the tyrannical reign of Emperor Nero who tries to gain his freedom by setting up his slave master’s son, Hero (Michael Crawford), with the apple of his eye, Philia (Annette Andre). With the jokes and gags coming a mile-a-minute, many of them landing, it’s a bawdy, gay ol’ time that also contains the final screen performance of Buster Keaton.
		
			
			
	Cleopatra (1963)
			
										
					Image Credit: Everett				
					
				
	More known for its production problems (newly-installed Fox chief Daryl Zanuck demanded the original cut be trimmed from four hours to 184 minutes), ballooning costs (production and marketing ran $44 million, or $481.8 million in 2026 dollars) and a torrid on-set affair between stars Elizabeth Taylor and Richard Burton, filmmaker Joseph Mankiewicz’s epic depicting queen Cleopatra VII of Egypt clashing with Mark Antony, Julius Caesar and the Romans, is a triumph of spectacle and production design, with some of the most extraordinary sets and costumes you’ll ever see. It was nominated for 9 Academy Awards, including Best Picture, winning Best Art Direction, Best Cinematography, Best Costume Design and Best Special Effects.
		
			
			
	300 (2007)
			
										
					Image Credit: Everett				
					
				
	As the story goes, Christopher Nolan was such a fan of Zack Snyder’s “300” and “Watchmen” that he convinced Warner Bros. to hire him to direct the Superman flick “Man of Steel,” even coming onboard as an executive producer. Based on Frank Miller’s comics, this CGI-heavy spectacle illustrating the Battle of Thermopylae in the Greco-Persian Wars, as 300 Spartans square off against Persian ruler Xerxes and his army of 300,000 soldiers, is heavy on testosterone and light on historical accuracy, featuring a ripped-and-roaring cast of warriors that includes Gerard Butler, David Wenham, Rodrigo Santoro and a young Michael Fassbender.
		
			
			
	Samson and Delilah (1949)
			
										
					Image Credit: Everett				
					
				
	When you think of a “sword-and-sandal film,” the first name that comes to mind is Cecil B. DeMille, the directorial maestro behind period epics “The King of Kings,” 1934’s “Cleopatra,” “The Ten Commandments,” and this drama about the biblical story of Samson (Victor Mature), an Israelite imbued with superhuman strength via his hair, and Delilah (Hedy Lamarr, fantastic), the woman hired by the Philistines to seduce him and uncover the source of his power. Here, DeMille has staged a sumptuous and pleasurable adaptation of Samson and Delilah bursting with colorful costumes and impressive scale that became the highest-grossing movie of 1950.
		
			
			
	History of the World, Part 1 (1981)
			
										
					Image Credit: Everett				
					
				
	Only two of the five chapters of Mel Brooks’ historical anthology-comedy qualify as “sword and sandal,” but they’re so damn funny that they had to be included on this list. There’s Mel Brooks as Moses (one of the five roles he plays in the film) carrying several stone tablets down from Mount Sinai, only to accidentally drop one of them and trim his Commandments from 15 to 10; and later, Brooks as Comicus, a stand-up comic in the Roman Empire who gets mixed up with Emperor Nero (Dom DeLuise), Empress Nympho (Madeline Kahn, brilliant) and Jesus (John Hurt). This is historical comedy at its finest whose reputation has only improved with age.
		
			
			
	Jason and the Argonauts (1963)
			
										
					Image Credit: Everett				
					
				
	Director Don Chaffey’s fantasy flick is a rite of passage for youngsters, boasting a number of awe-inspiring creature creations courtesy of stop-motion animation guru Ray Harryhausen as well as a rousing score from the great Bernard Herrmann. Who can forget Jason battling the Hydra, or the giant bronze statue of Talos that comes to life and throttles their ship, or the gigantic gods, or the climactic battle sequence between Jason and the skeletons (inspired by “The 7th Voyage of Sinbad”)? What a delightfully fun time this is.
		
			
			
	Cabiria (1914)
			
										
					Image Credit: Everett				
					
				
	Giovanni Pastrone’s Italian silent film epic was groundbreaking in its sense of grandiosity and its use of a moving camera, pushing cinema forward technologically, and made history as the first movie to ever be shown at the White House. It’s a series of five episodes chronicling the history of ancient Rome, adapted from Livy’s “Ab Urbe Condita,” told through the eyes of an aristocratic 8-year-old girl, Cabiria (Carolina Catena), who’s believed to be missing (and is subsequently abducted) after Mount Etna erupts. Martin Scorsese, an admirer of the film, screened a restored version at the 2006 Cannes Film Festival that prompted the late, great critic Roger Ebert to write, “The film was made with limitless scope and ambition, with towering sets and thousands of extras, with stunts that (because they were actually performed by stuntmen) have an impact lost in these days of visual effects.”
		
			
			
	Quo Vadis (1951)
			
										
					Image Credit: Everett				
					
				
	Directed by Mervyn LeRoy, producer of “The Wizard of Oz,” this historical epic about the end of Roman Emperor Nero’s reign of terror and his persecution of Christians, and starring Robert Taylor, Deborah Kerr and Peter Ustinov, is a triumph of sound and spectacle. Shot at Rome’s famed Cinecittà Studios, which “Ben-Hur” and “Cleopatra” later used, it was produced for $7 million by MGM — a record at the time — and grossed over $21 million, rescuing the studio from bankruptcy. It was nominated for 8 Academy Awards, including Best Picture, and features Elizabeth Taylor, Sophia Loren and Christopher Lee in blink-and-you’ll-miss-it turns.
		
			
			
	William Shakespeare’s Julius Caesar (1953)
			
										
					Image Credit: Everett				
					
				
	Joseph L. Mankiewicz’s adaptation of Shakespeare’s play “Julius Caesar” offers excellent costuming and production design, but is most noteworthy for its outstanding performances, led by Marlon Brando’s riveting turn as Mark Antony, resulting in his third consecutive Academy Award nomination for Best Actor. James Mason (Brutus), John Gielgud (Cassius) and Deborah Kerr (Portia) also impress.  
		
			
			
	Gladiator (2000)
			
										
					Image Credit: Everett				
					
				
	Winner of five Academy Awards, including Best Picture, Best Actor (for Russell Crowe) and Best Costume Design (Janty Yates), Ridley Scott’s historical epic tells the fictional tale of Maximus Decimus Meridius (Crowe), a Roman general who is set to succeed Emperor Marcus Aurelius when he is betrayed by Aurelius’ sleazy son Commodus (Joaquin Phoenix), who assassinates his own father, claims the throne and murders Maximus’ wife and child. Maximus is then sold into slavery, where he competes as a gladiator in the Roman arena, all the while plotting his revenge. Expertly directed by Scott, the film is carried to great heights by the acting brilliance of Crowe and Phoenix, who make perfect foils.
		
			
			
	The Prince of Egypt (1998)
			
										
					Image Credit: Everett				
					
				
	This DreamWorks animated musical about the life of Moses (voiced by Val Kilmer), who leads the enslaved Hebrews out of Egypt, offers an entrancing mix of stunning animated imagery, an excellent voice cast (Kilmer is joined by Ralph Fiennes, Michelle Pfeiffer, Sandra Bullock, Jeff Goldblum, Danny Glover, Patrick Stewart, Steve Martin and Martin Short), and memorable music, with songs written by Stephen Schwartz (“Enchanted,” “Wicked”) and a score from the inimitable Hans Zimmer. It spawned the hit single “When You Believe,” from Mariah Carey and Whitney Houston, which won the Academy Award for Best Original Song.
		
			
			
	Spartacus (1960)
			
										
					Image Credit: Everett				
					
				
	It’s one of the weaker films in director Stanley Kubrick’s celebrated canon (which says a lot about Kubrick), but still a thrilling slice of entertainment nevertheless. It centers on Spartacus (Kirk Douglas), a gladiator and slave who leads an army of fugitives and slaves against the corrupt leaders of the Roman Republic in the first century BC. On top of Kubrick’s direction and fantastic lensing by cinematographer Russell Metty, the performances from Douglas, Laurence Olivier (as the villainous/bisexual Roman senator Marcus Licinius Crassus), and Peter Ustinov as Spartacus’ gladiator mentor Batiatus are top-notch.
		
			
			
	The Odyssey (2026)
			
										
					Image Credit: Melinda Sue Gordon				
					
				
	Though it deviates from Homer’s tale (often in compelling ways), Christopher Nolan’s adaptation of “The Odyssey” is an extraordinary cinematic spectacle — made to be viewed in Imax 70mm, though good luck getting tickets — that, with its transfixing practical effects and sense of scale, feels like a soothing balm amid all the AI slop being bandied about. Come for Nolan’s masterful staging and direction and stay for the riveting turns from Matt Damon as Odysseus, Anne Hathaway as Penelope, Robert Pattinson as the scheming Antinous and Samantha Morton as the sorceress Circe.
		
			
			
	The Ten Commandments (1956)
			
										
					Image Credit: Everett				
					
				
	There’s a reason why Cecil B. DeMille’s masterpiece has aired around Passover on primetime network television in the U.S. every year since 1973: it’s a magnificent retelling of the life of Moses (Charlton Heston), an adopted Egyptian prince who frees the Hebrews from Egyptian slavery, leads their Exodus to Mount Sinai, then receives the Ten Commandments from God. Shot on location in Egypt and on gigantic sound stages, you can’t avert your eyes from DeMille’s lavish production, which was the most expensive film ever made at the time ($13 million), and grossed $122.7 million (or $1.51 billion today), making it the second highest-grossing film of the decade. Heston is fantastic, as is Yul Brynner as the antagonist Rameses II, Anne Baxter as Nefretiri and Edward G. Robinson, who steals every scene he’s in (in hilarious fashion) as the treacherous Dathan. Though the effects are dated, the parting of the Red Sea sequence will still make your jaw drop.
		
			
			
	Ben-Hur (1959)
			
										
					Image Credit: Everett				
					
				
	With all due respect to DeMille, Nolan and Kubrick, the no. 1 spot on the list is reserved for William Wyler’s fictional tale of Judah Ben-Hur (Charlton Heston, never better), an aristocratic Jewish prince who’s wrongly imprisoned by the Romans, sold into slavery where he becomes a champion charioteer, and then later witnesses the crucifixion of Jesus Christ. Wyler employed 10,000 extras and used 2,500 horses and 200 camels for the mammoth production, whose nine-minute chariot-racing sequence ranks among the greatest sequences in the history of cinema. “Ben-Hur” is recognized as one of the finest films ever made, and took home a record 11 Academy Awards, including Best Picture, Best Director and Best Actor.
		
	
	


	
		More from Variety
	
	
					

			
			
			
			
	
			
					

			
			
			
			
	
			
					

			
			
			
			
	
			
					

			
			
			
			
	
			
					

			
			
			
			
	
			
					

			
			
			
			
	
			
			
	
			
		

		

	
		More From Our Brands
	
	
		

			
	
			
			

	Rolling Stone

					Andrew and Tristan Tate Arrested in Miami, Await Extradition to U.K. on New Rape Charges		

					
				
			
			
	
			
	
		
			
	
			
			

	Robb Report

					A Luxe Sardinia Hotel Just Opened a New Penthouse Suite. Here’s a Look Inside.		

					
				
			
			
	
			
	
		
			
	
			
			

	Sportico

					Kerr of the Moment: Record Mile Attempt Is Months in Making

schema.headline: 20 Best Sword-and-Sandal Films, From ‘The Odyssey’ to ‘Gladiator’

title: Peru Earthquake Kills at Least 5, Leaves Hundreds Displaced
description:
Peru Earthquake
                          At Least 5 Dead & Hundreds Displaced
body:
Peru Earthquake
                          At Least 5 Dead & Hundreds Displaced
                      
                  
                                                                        Published 
                              July 19, 2026
                            8:50 AM PDT
                                                                            
      
    
                  
          
                          
                
                  
      
    
            A powerful earthquake tore through Peru's Andes region over the weekend ... leaving at least five people dead, more than 20 injured and hundreds forced from their homes, authorities say.
      
    
            Officials say the 5.5-magnitude quake struck Saturday night near the city of Sicaya in central Peru at a shallow depth of about 6 miles, triggering widespread damage across the region.
      
    
      
      #TemblorInternacionales 🇵🇪, un fuerte temblor de 5.5 ocurrió en Perú con epicentro al sur de Chupaca, el sismo causó varios daños en la ciudad de Huancayo, capital del departamento de Junín pic.twitter.com/s8Zrgv16Ry
              @wenewsec
          
  
    
            Authorities say several buildings partially collapsed, including a church and convent, while homes in some of the hardest-hit farming communities were reduced to rubble. Images from local TV showed residents huddled outside overnight as emergency crews searched damaged neighborhoods.
      

      
      Autoridades de #Perú informaron que el #Sismo de magnitud 5.1 con epicentro a 7 kilómetros al sur de Chupaca en la región Junín, provocó una intensidad IV-V en la zona y dejó varios puntos del distrito de Chilca en Huancayo sin energía eléctrica. Medios locales confirmaron la… pic.twitter.com/gi7fcqXTuz
              @daviddelapaz
  
    
            Peru's National Civil Defense Institute says at least 300 people have been displaced, and officials are still working to determine how many people remain missing.
      
    
            The country sits along the Pacific "Ring of Fire," one of the world's most seismically active regions, making earthquakes a frequent threat. In 2007, a devastating 7.9-magnitude quake killed nearly 600 people in southern Peru.

schema.headline: Peru Earthquake Kills at Least 5, Leaves Hundreds Displaced

body:
Ariana Grande's BF Ricky Alvarez
                          Spotted Dancing 'Side to Side' At Her NYC Concert
                      
                  
                                                                        Published 
                              July 19, 2026
                            6:48 AM PDT
                                                                            
      
    
                  
          
                          
                
                  
      
    
            Ricky Alvarez was front and center supporting girlfriend Ariana Grande over the weekend ... showing up at her concert and looking like he knew every word.
      
    
            Check out the video ... Ricky is seen dancing and singing along to Ariana's song "Imperfect for You," smiling as she performs for the packed crowd Saturday night at Barclays Center in Brooklyn.
      
    
          Waiting for your permission to load TikTok Post.
      
    
            His appearance comes after TMZ revealed Ariana and Ricky have rekindled their romance following her split from Ethan Slater.
      

                  
          
                          
                
                  
      
    
            As we previously reported, the two were recently spotted packing on the PDA during a stroll through New York City.
      
    
                  
          
                          
                
                  
      
    
            TMZ obtained photos showing Ariana wrapping an arm around Ricky while he grabbed a handful of her backside before the pair took a romantic walk through Central Park with her dog, Toulouse.
      
    
            Ariana and Ricky famously dated years ago before calling it quits, but it appears they've found their way back to each other.

            Here is an 18-year-old version of the young Hollywood actress showing off her bedazzled décolletage at "The Princess Diaries" Premiere back in 2001 (left).

            We know she serves elevated style and gives us just enough of that killer silhouette -- and leaves us wanting more -- but the question here is:
